dossiers.title: S3 promoted ansatz observable follow-up run
dossiers.purpose: Test whether the promoted structured Kuramoto-XY ansatz candidate retains its no-QPU observable behaviour under real backend transpilation, layout, and noise.
dossiers.hypothesis: If the structured candidate is hardware-usable, its measured observable proxies should remain directionally consistent with the exact statevector baseline under a matched simulator and backend layout package.
dossiers.falsification_condition: The candidate is falsified for hardware follow-up if transpilation depth, layout noise, or readout effects destroy the observed energy/synchronisation ordering relative to matched baseline candidates.
expected_observables: transpiled depth and two-qubit gate count

qpu_budget.recommended_initial_scope: transpile-only plus optional small shot-count observable run after approval

risks_and_confounds: The current observable row is not VQE optimisation.
Energy estimation may require many Pauli measurements if promoted beyond simulation.
The shallow resource winner may not be the best accuracy winner after noise.
Backend layout and readout can dominate the observable proxy.

decision_tree.positive: Promote to a small hardware observable comparison against matched ansatz baselines.
decision_tree.null: Keep as software-only design evidence and improve candidate generation.
decision_tree.negative: Reject this resource-ranked candidate for hardware follow-up and use accuracy-ranked candidates instead.

dossiers.paper_impact: Would support the S3 design-methods narrative by connecting no-QPU candidate selection to a bounded hardware-readiness package.
dossiers.follow_up_avenue: Provider-specific transpilation probes, then small observable runs only after budget approval.
possibilities_opened: observable-driven candidate rejection before expensive QPU use
dossiers.claim_boundary: This dossier does not authorise submission and does not claim VQE improvement, pulse-level performance, quantum advantage, or backend-independent behaviour.
prerequisites: attach exact measurement grouping before hardware execution

dossiers.title: S3 pulse-schedule feasibility follow-up run
dossiers.purpose: Test whether the hypergeometric Kuramoto-XY pulse schedule can be mapped to a provider-supported pulse-control or native-XY execution path after calibration review.
dossiers.hypothesis: If provider timing, pulse-count, and native interaction constraints are satisfied, a small calibrated pulse follow-up can test whether pulse shaping reduces digital Trotter overhead for the same observable family.
dossiers.falsification_condition: The pulse route is falsified for near-term hardware if provider calibration, timing granularity, pulse duration, or native interaction constraints cannot realise the schedule within the documented budget and error boundary.

qpu_budget.recommended_initial_scope: provider calibration review only; no pulse job before approval

risks_and_confounds: Metadata readiness is not calibration readiness.
Pulse schedules may require provider-specific waveform constraints not captured in metadata.
Native-XY analogue execution is not equivalent to a gate-level pulse schedule without a separate mapping.
The current analytic infidelity proxy is not a measured hardware error model.

decision_tree.positive: Prepare a provider-specific calibrated pulse preregistration package.
decision_tree.manual_review: Request platform-specific waveform constraints before any execution.
decision_tree.negative: Keep S3 pulse work as simulation-only and prioritise gate-based ansatz validation.

dossiers.paper_impact: Would define the bridge from S3 design methods to S4 multi-hardware pulse-level control.
dossiers.follow_up_avenue: S4 provider-specific pulse adapter and calibrated no-submit waveform package.
dossiers.claim_boundary: This dossier is metadata-only and does not calibrate pulses, open provider sessions, submit jobs, or establish hardware performance.
prerequisites: obtain provider-specific waveform and calibration constraints

claim_boundary: Dossiers document readiness only; they do not authorise hardware execution.
